message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 8
Recent Green phase: Added component building-block logic. Introduced constants COMPONENT_PREMIUM=25, BLOCK_SIZE=3, BLOCK_PREMIUM=60, COMPONENT_TYPES=Set(["rune"]); a `componentBasePremium(count)` helper (full blocks of 3 @60 + loose @25); and a `basePremium(items)` helper that splits component vs main items. runScenario now calls basePremium.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Note: basePremium currently filters the items array twice (once for components, once for main items) — consider whether a single pass is cleaner without hurting the metrics. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, compute APP mass before/after.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
## PRE Measurements

**ESLint smells**: 0 violations. Clean surface.
**Tests**: 8/8 passing.

### Naming Evaluation

- `componentBasePremium(count)` — computes base premium for component-type items via block pricing. Name reveals intent well.
- `basePremium(items)` — total base premium for all items. Clear.
- `runScenario(scenario)` — clear.
- `COMPONENT_TYPES` — set of types treated as components. Clear.

Naming is fine. No rename needed.

### APP Mass — `basePremium` (the flagged target)

```
const componentCount = items.filter((item) => COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)).length;
const mainTotal = items
  .filter((item) => !COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type))
  .reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);
return mainTotal + componentBasePremium(componentCount);
```
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: ~8 (componentCount, items, item×3, mainTotal, sum, item)
- Invocations: filter, has, .length(skip), filter, has, reduce, +, BASE_PREMIUM[], +, componentBasePremium → ~10 → 20
- Loops: 2 (two filters; reduce is the loop body of one) → counting filter + filter+reduce as 3 iteration constructs ≈ 3 → 15
- Assignments: 0

Mass ≈ 8 + 20 + 15 = **~43**. Two passes over the array.

### McCabe — `basePremium`
- Start: 1, no explicit branches (ternary/if/loop keywords). Predicates inside filter are functions but not decision keywords at this level.
- **McCabe: 1**

McCabe is already at the floor everywhere. The flagged issue is the **double filter pass** — a Rule 3 (duplication of the `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` predicate / iteration) and Rule 4 (fewest elements) concern, not a complexity-score concern.

### Refactoring: single-pass reduce

This removes the duplicated `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)` predicate and the second array traversal, collapsing two filters + reduce into one reduce.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: This change trades two functional passes for one imperative loop, but it introduces mutable assignments (`+= `) and an explicit `if/else`, which raises both APP mass (assignments at ×6) and McCabe (+1 for the branch). Let me measure to see whether the trade is worth it.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>/dev/null; echo "---TESTS---"; pnpm test 2>&1 | tail -6
input.description: Re-measure ESLint and tests POST change

content.text:
Tests still green, ESLint still clean. Now let me compute POST APP mass and McCabe on the new `basePremium`.

### POST APP Mass — `basePremium`
```
let mainTotal = 0;          // assignment(6) + const(1) + binding(1)
let componentCount = 0;     // assignment(6) + const(1) + binding(1)
for (const item of items) { // loop(5) + bindings(item, items)
  if (COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)) {  // conditional(4) + invocation has(2) + bindings
    componentCount += 1;    // assignment(6) + const(1)
  } else {
    mainTotal += BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]; // assignment(6) + invocation(2)
  }
}
return mainTotal + componentBasePremium(componentCount); // invocation(2)
```
- Assignments: 4 (two `let` inits + two `+=`) → 24
- Loop: 1 → 5
- Conditional: 1 → 4
- Invocations: has, BASE_PREMIUM[], +, componentBasePremium ≈ 5 → 10
- Constants: ~4 → 4
- Bindings: ~6 → 6

Mass ≈ 24 + 5 + 4 + 10 + 4 + 6 = **~53**.

### POST McCabe — `basePremium`
- Start: 1, `if/else`: +1 → **McCabe: 2**

## Decision: REVERT

Every measured signal got **worse**, not better:

- **APP mass**: ~43 → ~53 (the four mutable assignments at ×6 cost far more than the second filter pass they removed)
- **McCabe**: 1 → 2 (the explicit `if/else` introduced a decision point the functional version did not have)
- **ESLint smells**: 0 → 0 (no gain)
- **Cognitive complexity**: 0 → ~1 (added a branch)

The "single pass" is faster at runtime, but the metrics this workflow optimizes for all regressed. The duplication that was removed (one extra `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` predicate) was cheap; the mutation and branching that replaced it are expensive. Reverting to the functional version.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Rather than revert outright, I kept the functional structure but extracted the duplicated predicate `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)` into a named `isComponent` helper. This is the actual duplication the prompt flagged (Rule 3 — the predicate appeared twice), and the named helper also improves Rule 2 (reveals intent). Let me verify.

content.text:
Tests green, ESLint clean. Let me compute the final POST metrics on this version.

### POST APP Mass — `basePremium` (extracted predicate)
```
const isComponent = (item) => COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type);  // helper: invocation has(2), bindings(2) ≈ 4
const componentCount = items.filter(isComponent).length;       // bindings, filter(2), .length
const mainTotal = items
  .filter((item) => !isComponent(item))                        // filter(2), isComponent(2), !
  .reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);    // reduce(2), +, BASE_PREMIUM[](2), +
return mainTotal + componentBasePremium(componentCount);       // componentBasePremium(2), +
```
- Constants: 1 (the `0`)
- Bindings: ~9
- Invocations: ~10 → 20
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 2 iteration constructs (filter, filter+reduce) → ~3 → 15
- Assignments: 0 (all `const`)

Mass for `basePremium` body ≈ unchanged at **~43**; the extracted `isComponent` adds a tiny constant helper (~4) but removes the inline `COMPONENT_TYPES.has` duplication.

### POST McCabe — `basePremium`
- Start: 1, no decision keywords → **McCabe: 1** (unchanged, still at floor)

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied**: Extracted the duplicated `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type)` predicate into a named `isComponent(item)` helper.

This was chosen after an experiment-and-revert:

1. **First attempt** — single-pass `for` loop (as the prompt suggested). Measured and **reverted**: it traded the cheap second filter for four mutable assignments and an `if/else`, regressing APP mass (~43 → ~53), McCabe (1 → 2), and cognitive complexity (0 → 1). The numbers, not feel, killed it.
2. **Kept change** — extract the shared predicate. Addresses the real duplication (Rule 3, the predicate appeared twice) and improves intent (Rule 2) without adding any branch or mutation.

**Deltas (function `basePremium`)**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (already clean, no regression)
- Cognitive complexity: 0 → 0
- APP mass: ~43 → ~43 (flat; helper offsets removed inline predicate)
- McCabe cyclomatic: 1 → 1 (at floor, preserved)

**Tests**: 8/8 passing.

The key finding for the record: the suggested single-pass rewrite was measurably worse on every score this workflow tracks, because functional `filter`/`reduce` carries no conditional/assignment APP cost while the imperative loop does. The duplication was removed the cheaper way — by naming the shared predicate.

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
